learning_objectives: Define causal, noncausal, physical, and anticipative systems using input time dependence.
Recognize whether a system uses present, past, or future input values.
Use time-shift formulas such as x(t-2), x(t+2), x(-t), and y(t+1)=x(t) to decide causality.
Explain why noncausal systems can still be useful with prerecorded data, spatial variables, or output delay.

visual_plan.rationale: Use the textbook Figure 1.30 as the canonical static visual because it directly shows an input, a noncausal output that starts before the input, and the delayed causal version. Add a React/Canvas time-shift demo because causality is often confused when students see expressions like x(t+1), x(t-1), x(-t), or y(t+1)=x(t). Do not use generated images because the textbook already provides the main diagram and symbolic/interactive rendering is more precise for time-shift logic.
visual_plan.cram: Use visuals to quickly spot red-flag future dependence: any required input time greater than the output time.
visual_plan.standard: Use Figure 1.30 and one time-shift demo to connect the definition, a representative example, and exam-style decisions.
visual_plan.top_score: Use the demo to distinguish subtle cases such as y(t+1)=x(t), time reversal, and delay-based realization.

blocks.instruction: Render Page 1 as a compact outline only. Title it 'Section Objective' and write one sentence: 'Decide whether a system can operate in real time by checking whether its output needs future input values.' Then title a second short list 'Concepts In This Section' and list only these concept names, with no explanations: causal system; noncausal system; future input dependence; time-shift causality test; delayed realization.

blocks.latex: y(t_0)\text{ is causal only if it depends on }x(t)\text{ for }t\le t_0.
blocks.explanation_instruction: Start the page with the heading '## 1. Causal means: no future input'. Explain in 100–130 words that at output time \(t_0\), a real-time system may use present input \(x(t_0)\) and past input values \(x(t)\) with \(t<t_0\), but not future values with \(t>t_0\). Define every symbol: \(y(t_0)\) is the output now, \(x(t)\) is the input at time \(t\), and \(t_0\) is the decision time. Include one minimal example: \(y(t)=x(t)+x(t-1)\) is causal. Exam trigger: check whether the formula asks for input at a time larger than the output time. Common misuse: thinking that any delay is noncausal; delays are usually causal because they use past input.

mode_specific_visual_use.cram: Use the early output pulse as the instant visual cue for noncausality.
mode_specific_visual_use.standard: Connect each waveform to the equation and notice which output happens before the input exists.
mode_specific_visual_use.top_score: Use the delayed waveform to see how an anticipative system can be realized after waiting.

blocks.caption_instruction: One sentence: Figure 1.30 shows an input pulse, a noncausal output that starts before the input, and a delayed output that becomes causal.
blocks.description_instruction: Describe the three plots in 2–3 sentences. Emphasize that the input pulse occurs from t=0 to t=1, while the output in part (b) includes a pulse from t=-2 to t=-1 before the input is applied. Then point out that part (c) shifts the output right so that no response occurs before the needed input is available.

blocks.explanation_instruction: Start the page with the heading '## 2. A future term makes the system noncausal'. Explain in 110–150 words. Say that \(x(t-2)\) is safe because it asks for the input 2 seconds before time \(t\), but \(x(t+2)\) is the problem because it asks for the input 2 seconds after time \(t\). Tie this directly to Figure 1.30: the output can start before the input pulse begins because the equation is using future knowledge. State when to use this formula: when an exam asks whether a time-shifted input-output rule is physically realizable in real time. Common misuse: treating both \(t-2\) and \(t+2\) as just 'shifts' without asking whether the required input time is in the future.

mode_specific_visual_use.cram: Use the red highlighted future sample to decide causal versus noncausal fast.
mode_specific_visual_use.standard: Move the time marker and connect each formula to the input sample it requires.
mode_specific_visual_use.top_score: Test tricky cases like reflection and equations written with shifted output time.

blocks.instruction: Build a React + Canvas demo titled 'Causality Time-Shift Tester'. Show a simple input waveform x(t) on a horizontal time axis and a movable vertical marker labeled 'output time t'. Provide selectable rules: y(t)=x(t-a), y(t)=x(t+a), y(t)=x(-t), and y(t+a)=x(t). Include a slider for a from 0 to 3. When a rule is selected, draw an arrow from output time t to the input time required by the rule. Color the arrow green if the required input time is ≤ the output time and red if it is > the output time. For y(t+a)=x(t), also show the rewritten form y(s)=x(s-a) after substituting s=t+a. Add a one-line status label: 'Causal: uses present/past input only' or 'Noncausal: needs future input'. Keep the demo visually clean and lecture-note styled; no decorative graphics.

blocks.explanation_instruction: Start the page with the heading '## 3. Worked example: do not judge before aligning the output time'. Use Example 1.13 as the representative worked example. Target 130–160 words. Explain that \(y(t)=x(-t)\) is noncausal because a future impulse can appear as an earlier output after reflection; \(y(t)=x(t+1)\) is noncausal because output at \(t\) needs input at \(t+1\). Then focus on the displayed formula: rewrite it by setting \(s=t+1\), so \(y(s)=x(s-1)\). This output at time \(s\) uses input one second in the past, so it is causal. Exam note: if the output side is shifted, rewrite the equation until it is in the form \(y(\text{output time})=x(\text{input time})\). Common trap: seeing \(+1\) and automatically calling it noncausal.

Start the page with the heading '## 4. Noncausal systems can still matter if delay is allowed'. Explain in 120–160 words. First state that the drill system \(y(t)=\int_{t-5}^{t+5}x(\tau)\,d\tau\) is noncausal because output at time \(t\) needs input values up to \(t+5\), which are future values. Then explain the displayed delayed version: if we wait 5 seconds and output \(\hat y(t)=y(t-5)\), the newest input needed is only \(x(t)\), so the delayed system is causal. Define \(\tau\) as the integration variable. Also mention the two reasons noncausal systems are studied: prerecorded data can make future samples available, and non-time variables such as space do not have the same real-time causality restriction. Common misuse: saying noncausal means useless; it means not directly real-time unless data or delay makes it possible.

blocks.instruction: Create a recap page titled '📌 Key Takeaways'. Include 4 bullets, each ≤24 words, and include formulas explicitly. Required formulas: \(y(t_0)\) causal only if it depends on \(x(t)\) for \(t\le t_0\); \(y(t)=x(t-2)+x(t+2)\) is noncausal because of \(x(t+2)\); \(y(t+1)=x(t)\Rightarrow y(s)=x(s-1)\) is causal; \(\hat y(t)=y(t-5)=\int_{t-10}^{t}x(\tau)d\tau\) is the delayed causal version of \(y(t)=\int_{t-5}^{t+5}x(\tau)d\tau\). End with one bridge sentence: 'Next, use causality together with other system properties to classify systems faster and more accurately.'

knowledge_points.label: Causal systems use present and past input only

questions.stem: At time t0, which input values may a causal real-time system use?
options: A. Only x(t0), never past values
B. x(t) for t ≤ t0
C. x(t) for t ≥ t0
D. Any x(t), as long as the formula is linear
questions.explanation: A causal system can depend on present and past input values, but not future input values.

wrong_option_explanations.A: Memory is allowed in causal systems; past values are not forbidden.
wrong_option_explanations.C: This includes future values, which breaks real-time causality.
wrong_option_explanations.D: Linearity does not decide causality; time dependence does.

questions.hint: Ask: what input values are already available at time t0?

questions.explanation: The output uses the present input x(t) and a past input x(t-2), so it is causal.

wrong_option_explanations.A: x(t+3) is a future input value.
wrong_option_explanations.C: For many t values, 2−t can be greater than t, so the system can require future input.
wrong_option_explanations.D: The x(t+1) term requires future input.

questions.hint: Look for any input time greater than the output time t.

knowledge_points.label: Visual recognition of noncausal output before input

questions.stem: In Figure 1.30, why does the output in part (b) prove noncausality?
options: A. The output has two pulses instead of one
B. One output pulse occurs before the input pulse is applied
C. The output pulse height is 1
D. The input pulse lasts only one second
questions.explanation: A real-time causal system cannot produce an output before the input information needed for that output is available.

wrong_option_explanations.A: Multiple pulses can be causal or noncausal depending on timing.
wrong_option_explanations.D: Pulse duration alone does not determine causality.

questions.hint: Compare the first time where x(t) is nonzero with the first time where y(t) is nonzero.

knowledge_points.label: Equation (1.26) contains one past term and one future term

questions.stem: For y(t)=x(t−2)+x(t+2), which term makes the system noncausal?
options: A. x(t−2), because it is delayed
B. x(t+2), because it requires future input
C. Both terms, because any shift breaks causality
D. Neither term, because the system is just adding two samples
questions.explanation: At time t, x(t+2) is not known yet in real-time operation.

wrong_option_explanations.A: x(t−2) is a past input value, so it is allowed in a causal system.
wrong_option_explanations.C: Past shifts are allowed; future shifts are the problem.
wrong_option_explanations.D: Addition does not remove the future dependence.

questions.hint: At output time t, which input sample has not happened yet?

questions.stem: A system has y(t)=x(t−4)+2x(t). Which statement is correct?
options: A. It is causal because it uses past and present input only
B. It is noncausal because x(t−4) is shifted
C. It is noncausal because the coefficient 2 changes the input
D. It is causal only if x(t) is a rectangular pulse
questions.explanation: Both x(t−4) and x(t) are available at time t, so the system is causal.

wrong_option_explanations.B: A delay uses past input and does not break causality.
wrong_option_explanations.C: Changing amplitude does not determine causality.
wrong_option_explanations.D: Causality is a property of the system rule, not a specific input shape.

questions.hint: A shifted term is not automatically bad; identify whether it is past or future.

knowledge_points.label: Rewrite shifted-output equations before judging causality

questions.stem: For y(t+1)=x(t), which conclusion is correct?
options: A. Noncausal, because +1 appears in y(t+1)
B. Causal, because rewriting with s=t+1 gives y(s)=x(s−1)
C. Noncausal, because x(t) is always future input
D. Causal only if x(t) is an impulse
questions.explanation: After aligning the output time as s, the required input is x(s−1), which is one second in the past.

wrong_option_explanations.A: The +1 is on the output side, so you must rewrite before judging.
wrong_option_explanations.C: x(t) is not future relative to the original output time t+1.
wrong_option_explanations.D: The decision does not depend on a special input shape.

questions.hint: Rename the actual output time first.

questions.stem: Rewrite y(t+2)=x(t−1) in the form y(s)=x(____), then decide whether it is causal.
questions.ideal_answer: Let s=t+2, so t=s−2. Then y(s)=x(s−3). It is causal because the output at time s uses input from three seconds earlier.
grading_rubric: Must define s as the actual output time t+2
Must state that the system is causal because it uses past input
questions.explanation: This checks whether the student can handle equations where the output time is not written as y(t).
questions.hint: Set s equal to the time inside y( ).

knowledge_points.label: Noncausal systems can be realized with output delay

questions.stem: The system y(t)=∫ from t−5 to t+5 of x(τ)dτ is noncausal. Which delayed output is causal?
options: A. ŷ(t)=y(t+5)=∫ from t to t+10 of x(τ)dτ
B. ŷ(t)=y(t−5)=∫ from t−10 to t of x(τ)dτ
C. ŷ(t)=y(t)=∫ from t−5 to t+5 of x(τ)dτ
D. ŷ(t)=−y(t)
questions.explanation: Delaying the output by 5 seconds shifts the integration window so the newest required input is x(t), not a future value.

wrong_option_explanations.A: This advances the output and still requires future input.
wrong_option_explanations.C: This is the original noncausal system.
wrong_option_explanations.D: Changing the sign does not remove future dependence.

questions.hint: The upper integration limit must be no greater than the current output time.

knowledge_points.label: Noncausal systems are useful with prerecorded data or non-time variables

questions.stem: Which situation can make a noncausal system practically meaningful?
options: A. Real-time control where future input is unknown
B. Prerecorded signal processing where future samples are already stored
C. Any system with a nonlinear equation
D. Any system with no memory
questions.explanation: If the full input record is already available, future samples relative to a chosen time t can be accessed from storage.

wrong_option_explanations.A: Real-time operation cannot know future input values.
wrong_option_explanations.C: Nonlinearity is unrelated to whether future input is available.
wrong_option_explanations.D: A memoryless system can be causal, but that does not explain noncausal usefulness.

questions.hint: Ask whether the system has to operate live or can use stored data.

page-105.caption: Input–output of a noncausal system and the causal output achieved by delay.
page-105.description: The figure has three time-domain plots with horizontal axis t and vertical labels x(t), y(t), and ŷ(t). In (a), the input x(t) is a unit-height rectangular pulse from t=0 to t=1; in (b), the output y(t) consists of two unit pulses from t=-2 to -1 and from t=2 to 3, showing one response occurs before the input and is therefore noncausal. In (c), the delayed output ŷ(t) shifts the pulses to t=0 to 1 and t=4 to 5, illustrating how adding delay makes the output causal. Students should notice the timing relationship between input and output and how delay removes the advance response.

page-105.caption: Example 1.13 asks students to assess the causality of three continuous-time systems: y(t)=x(−t), y(t)=x(t+1), and y(t+1)=x(t).
page-105.description: A boxed textbook example titled "Example 1.13 Assessing System Causality" presents three continuous-time system equations to evaluate: (a) y(t)=x(-t), (b) y(t)=x(t+1), and (c) y(t+1)=x(t). There are no plotted axes; the figure is a text panel with labeled parts (a), (b), and (c), plus a footnote stating that in real-time operation the response is essentially simultaneous with the input. Students should notice the time reversal and time shifts in the input-output relationships and relate them to whether the output depends on present, past, or future input values.

web_sources.snippet: an epistemic concept of causality in order to distinguish between causal and noncausal relations. The contemporary philosophical literature on causality
